---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## From an 8% Average to a Paper

- Part 12 — the arc the eight weeks walked, and the closing sentence

::: notes
Welcome back to The Eight-Minute Presentation and the Replication Packet, part twelve of twelve — the final segment of the chapter, and of the camp. In part eleven we said reproducibility is the form your credibility takes. Now we close by tracing the arc the eight weeks actually walked — from "the average return was eight percent" to a defensible, reproducible paper — and naming the one sentence the whole camp existed to let you say.
:::

## The Arc the Eight Weeks Walked

- Week 1: a number became a number-*with-uncertainty*
- Week 2: "we control for X" is a claim, not a spell
- Weeks 3–4: designs — IV, RD, diff-in-diff, synthetic control
- Weeks 5–6: read as a skeptic; sharpen the inference
- Week 7: made it yours; Week 8: communicable and checkable

::: notes
Trace the arc the eight weeks actually walked. Week one turned a number into a number-with-uncertainty — you found the average return was about eight percent, and learned it had a standard error, a survivorship filter, hidden assumptions. Week two turned correlation into the machinery of regression and taught you that "we control for X" is a claim, not a spell — the ghost of omitted-variable bias. Weeks three and four turned that claim into designs: instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences, synthetic control — each a specific argument for why a comparison is fair, each with its own named way of failing. Week five taught you to read other people's papers as a skeptic, the same skill as reading your own honestly. Week six sharpened the inference — the right standard errors, the multiple-testing correction, the bootstrap. Week seven made it yours: an idea worth pursuing, data pulled reproducibly, a pre-analysis plan tagged before you could fool yourself, an analysis dataset built without look-ahead, an identification memo naming every threat. And Week eight made it communicable and checkable — written up with calibrated verbs, robustness that survived, peer-reviewed, defended in eight minutes, and packaged so a stranger can rebuild every claim.
:::

## The Sentence the Camp Existed For

- Not "the average return was eight percent" anymore
- A question nobody had cleanly answered
- A design you can defend sentence by sentence
- Robust, honestly uncertain, reproducible with one command
- "What I found, why believe it, the command that checks me"

::: notes
What you have at the end is not "the average return was eight percent." It is a question nobody had cleanly answered, attacked with a design you can defend sentence by sentence, estimated with honest uncertainty, robust to the choices you worried about, written so a referee believes it, and packaged so a skeptic can reproduce it with one command. That is an original empirical paper. That is what a researcher does. The eight percent was the toe in the water; this is the swim. The single sentence that separates the two — the one this whole camp existed to let you say truthfully — is: "Here is what I found, here is exactly why you should believe it, and here is the command that lets you check me." The two deliverables that close the camp are the same project from its two surfaces: the final manuscript build, where your write-up becomes a P-D-F with tables and figures generated by code, and the final compile and dry-run, where you assemble the six-part packet and rehearse until the talk fits in seven minutes. Then read the capstone gallery for its seams. You can say the sentence now. Go say it.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Across eight weeks, **a number became a number-with-uncertainty**, then a controlled-for *claim*, then a defended *design*, then a skeptically-read and sharply-inferred result, then your own pre-registered project — and finally something *communicable and checkable*. What you have is an original empirical paper. The sentence the camp was for: *"Here is what I found, here is why you should believe it, and here is the command that checks me."* You can say it now. Go say it.
:::

::: notes
To close the chapter and the camp: trace the arc the eight weeks walked. Week one turned a raw eight-percent average into a number-with-uncertainty. Week two made "we control for X" a claim, not a spell. Weeks three and four built real designs — instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences, synthetic control. Weeks five and six taught skeptical reading and sharper inference. Week seven made the project yours, pre-registered and reproducibly built. And Week eight made it communicable and checkable, defended in eight minutes and packaged for a stranger. What you have is no longer the average return was eight percent; it is an original empirical paper — a clean question, a defensible design, honest uncertainty, surviving robustness, and a one-command rebuild. The sentence the whole camp existed to let you say truthfully is: here is what I found, here is why you should believe it, and here is the command that lets you check me. You can say it now. Go say it.
:::
